chunks.text: Greedy Algorithms Prof. Timothy Sun Cashier’s algorithm “Take biggest possible coin until you’re done.” Cashier’s algorithm “Take biggest possible coin until you’re done.” Claim: matches DP solution Cashier’s algorithm “Take biggest possible coin until you’re done.” Claim: matches DP solution (for this currency) Cashier’s algorithm In an optimal solution, you can’t have more than 4 pennies: Cashier’s algorithm The greedy choice can be shown to always be correct with many exchanges: Cashier’s algorithm The greedy choice can be shown to always be correct with many exchanges: #P ≤ 4 #N ≤ 1 #D ≤ 2 #N < 1 or #D < 2 Cashier’s algorithm Why is the cashier’s algorithm not optimal for 1¢, 5¢, 7¢? Cashier’s algorithm Why is the cashier’s algorithm not optimal for 1¢, 5¢, 7¢? 10 = 5 + 5 (optimal) = 7 + 1 + 1 + 1 (greedy) Backtracking vs. Greedy Backtracking: try every branch Backtracking vs. Greedy Backtracking: try every branch Greedy: try the “best-looking” branch (often, just a heuristic) Greedy TSP Traveling Salesperson Problem: “What’s the quickest way to visit every city and come back to where you started?” Greedy TSP Nearest neighbor heuristic ● “Go to closest unvisited city.” Very rarely produces the optimal solution. Greedy TSP In the following conﬁguration, how does nearest-neighbor perform? Greedy TSP Proving correctness Backtracking considers all branches, but greedy algorithms only consider one: ● Greedy choice #1 ○ Greedy choice #2 ■ Greedy choice #3 ● Greedy choice #4 ○ ... Proving correctness 1. “Induction” a. There is an optimal solution containing greedy choice #1 b. Recurse and use induction 2. “Well-ordering” a. Look at ﬁrst difference between greedy and optimal solutions b. The optimal solution can actually use the greedy choice here Proving correctness 1. “Induction” a. There is an optimal solution containing greedy choice #1 b. Recurse and use induction 2. “Well-ordering” a. Look at ﬁrst difference between greedy and optimal solutions b. The optimal solution can actually use the greedy choice here Both approaches use an exchange argument. “Unweighted” 0-1 Knapsack Suppose you have a bag with capacity C and a set of items with weights w1 < w2 < w3 < … < wn Maximize the number of items that can be placed into the bag without exceeding C. “Unweighted” 0-1 Knapsack Greedy algorithm: pick w1, w2, w3… until you run out of space. “Unweighted” 0-1 Knapsack Greedy algorithm: pick w1, w2, w3… until you run out of space. Proof: (“Induction” method) consider any optimal solution. ● If w1 is in it, we’re done. ● If not, exchange any element with w1. File Storage On a magnetic tape, data is found via linear search. What’s the best way of storing, minimizing average search time (average starting index)? File Storage Depending on the order, you can have different search times. File Storage The greedy algorithm (smallest → largest) wins! File Storage Why does the greedy approach work? “Sorting” reduces cost: Ski rentals When choosing skis, the length should be close to your height. Ski rentals When choosing skis, the length should be close to your height. Given people’s heights P[1, … , n] and ski lengths S[1, … , n], what’s the best way of assigning skis so that the average difference between ski length and person height is minimized? Ski rentals When

greedy algorithm (smallest → largest) wins! File Storage Why does the greedy approach work? “Sorting” reduces cost: Ski rentals When choosing skis, the length should be close to your height. Ski rentals When choosing skis, the length should be close to your height. Given people’s heights P[1, … , n] and ski lengths S[1, … , n], what’s the best way of assigning skis so that the average difference between ski length and person height is minimized? Ski rentals When choosing skis, the length should be close to your height. Given people’s heights P[1, … , n] and ski lengths S[1, … , n], what’s the best way of assigning skis so that the average difference between ski length and person height is minimized? Greedy algorithm is again sorting, but why does it work? Ski rentals The same “swap” argument holds: given skiers A and B, and skis C and D, the greedy way should be at least as good as the non-greedy way: Ski rentals (D-A) + (B-C) = [(D-B)+(B-C)+(C-A)] + (B-C) ≥ (D-B) + (C-A) Ski rentals Two other cases to consider (others are symmetric): Class scheduling Given a set of classes with start and ﬁnish times: (s1, f1), (s2, f2), …, (sn, fn) Maximize the number of classes you can take without overlap. Class scheduling Greedy algorithm #1: pick shortest class Greedy algorithm #2: pick longest class Greedy algorithm #3: pick earliest starting class Greedy algorithm #4: pick earliest ﬁnishing class ...then remove any overlaps and recurse Class scheduling Counterexamples for #1-#3: The correct approach Greedy algorithm #4: pick earliest ﬁnishing class The correct approach Greedy algorithm #4: pick earliest ﬁnishing class Proof: another exchange argument, with key idea: In a valid schedule, the ﬁrst class ends before any other class begins. Fire drills The university wants to schedule ﬁre drills so that every class can practice their evacuation plan. What’s the fewest number of drills that need to be scheduled? Fire drills The university wants to schedule ﬁre drills so that every class can practice their evacuation plan. What’s the fewest number of drills that need to be scheduled? Use the same algorithm. Proof by duality The number of ﬁre drills can’t be less than the number of classes in a schedule. Proof by duality The number of ﬁre drills can’t be less than the number of classes in a schedule. This plan uses the same number of drills as classes in our optimal schedule. Assigning classrooms The university now wants to ﬁgure out which classes should be in which classrooms. What’s the fewest number of classrooms needed? Assigning classrooms What would be a greedy way of assigning a color/room?? Assigning classrooms What would be a greedy way of assigning a color/room?? ● When trying to color a class, give it the “smallest” color/room. A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: …but sorting by starting time does work! Assigning classrooms When going by increasing starting time, each time you need color k, there are k classes going on at the same time. Assigning classrooms When going by increasing starting

coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: A suboptimal coloring strategy The same approach from earlier doesn’t work: …but sorting by starting time does work! Assigning classrooms When going by increasing starting time, each time you need color k, there are k classes going on at the same time. Assigning classrooms When going by increasing starting time, each time you need color k, there are k classes going on at the same time. Can’t use fewer than k colors, so it must be optimal. Interval graphs This is a special instance of an NP-hard problem called graph coloring. For general graphs, it’s harder to solve these problems. Interval graphs Text compression Given a long piece of text, want to compress the text: ● char primitive in Java: 2 bytes = 16 bits Text compression Given a long piece of text, want to compress the text: ● char primitive in Java: 2 bytes = 16 bits 1. Not every Unicode character used 2. Letter frequencies Text compression Suppose text only uses capital letters ● 26 characters → 5 bits ○ A = 00000 ○ B = 00001 ○ C = 00010 ○ etc. Text compression To encode, replace each char with its codeword: “BCAD” → 00001000100000000011 Text compression To encode, replace each char with its codeword: “BCAD” → 00001000100000000011 Doesn’t take into account letter frequencies Decoding ● With a ﬁxed-length codewords, easy to decode: 00001 00010 00000 00011 Decoding ● With a ﬁxed-length codewords, easy to decode: 00001 00010 00000 00011 ● How to decode varying-length codes? Preﬁx(-Free) Code Make sure no code word is a preﬁx of another: A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 11010001011 uniquely decodes into CABAABC Preﬁx Codes as Binary Trees Every preﬁx code can be converted into a “full” binary tree. A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 Preﬁx Codes as Binary Trees Every preﬁx code can be converted into a “full” binary tree. Which chars should be close to the root, and which should be far? A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 Preﬁx Codes as Binary Trees Every preﬁx code can be converted into a “full” binary tree. Which chars should be close to the root, and which should be far? Common = close, uncommon = far A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 Preﬁx Codes as Binary Trees “ABCACCBAA”: f(A) = 4, f(B) = 2, f(C) = 3 A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 “ABCACCBAA” → 01011011111000 (14 bits) Preﬁx Codes as Binary Trees “ABCACCBAA”: f(A) = 4, f(B) = 2, f(C) = 3 A = 0, B = 10, C = 11 “ABCACCBAA” → 01011011111000 (14 bits) Claim: best possible encoding length for this word A greedy algorithm 1. Make a node with each character and its frequency. A greedy algorithm 1. Make a node with each character and its frequency. “FFCBCFCBDDAFEBFFBDFBBFBACFFACAABA” → A greedy algorithm 1. Make a node with each character and its frequency. 2. Until there’s one tree left: a. Make a new tree with the two smallest trees as subtrees, adding their frequencies. “FFCBCFCBDDAFEBFFBDFBBFBACFFACAABA” → Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman

Make a new tree with the two smallest trees as subtrees, adding their frequencies. “FFCBCFCBDDAFEBFFBDFBBFBACFFACAABA” → Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding Huffman Coding A = 11 B = 10 C = 010 D = 0110 E = 0111 F = 00 Huffman Coding A = 11 B = 10 C = 010 D = 0110 E = 0111 F = 00 Huffman Coding A = 11 B = 10 C = 010 D = 0110 E = 0111 F = 00 Total length: 6(2)+8(2)+5(3)+3(4)+1(4)+10(2) = 78 Huffman Coding How do we eﬃciently determine the least frequent trees? Huffman Coding How do we eﬃciently determine the least frequent trees? Use a priority queue Huffman Coding In a binary heap, insert and extract-min take O(log n) time. Huffman Coding In a binary heap, insert and extract-min take O(log n) time. Total runtime: O(n log n) time Huffman Coding Huffman codes try to optimize total codeword length: Huffman Coding Huffman codes try to optimize total codeword length: Each character c is converted into a codeword of length d(c), which appears f(c) times. Huffman codes try to optimize total codeword length: Each character c is converted into a codeword of length d(c), which appears f(c) times. Huffman’s algorithm produces an optimal preﬁx code. (but not every optimal code comes from Huffman’s algorithm) Huffman Coding “Optimality” No assumptions about input besides frequencies: ● QU in English “Optimality” Better compression for the following string: “AAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCD AAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCDAAAAAABBCCCCDA AAAAABBCCCCD” “AAAAAABBCCCCD 9 times” Correctness Suppose A and B are the two least common characters. Claim: In some optimal tree, A and B are siblings (greedy choice). Greedy choice If A is not at the deepest layer, how to improve the cost? Greedy choice If A is not at the deepest layer, how to improve the cost? Swap A to the deepest layer. Greedy choice What if A and B are not siblings? Greedy choice What if A and B are not siblings? Do another swap (doesn’t change cost). Greedy choice What if A and B are not siblings? Do another swap (doesn’t change cost). Now they’re siblings. Inductive Step?
